blogs.title: What is Event Loop?
blogs.shortDesc: The Event Loop is the mechanism that allows JavaScript to perform non-blocking asynchronous operations despite being single-threaded. It constantly checks the call stack and the callback queue and pushes pending callbacks into the stack once it becomes empty, enabling tasks like setTimeout, promises, and async/await to run efficiently.

blogs.title: What is React?
blogs.shortDesc: React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces using a component-based architecture. It updates the UI efficiently using a virtual DOM and supports reusable components, declarative programming, and unidirectional data flow, making it ideal for fast and scalable web applications.

blogs.title: What is true - false
blogs.shortDesc: JavaScript automatically converts values into booleans when required; some values are considered truthy (like [], {}, 'text', numbers except 0) while others are falsy (like 0, '', null, undefined, NaN, false). Understanding these helps avoid unexpected comparison results and bugs.

blogs.title: What is false - true
blogs.shortDesc: Truthy and falsy evaluation affects conditions and equality comparisons. For example, `!!value` converts values to a boolean form. JavaScript coercion rules automatically convert operands, which explains why unexpected equality results occur during loose comparison (`==`).

Due to floating-point binary representation limitations in IEEE-754 format, values like 0.1 and 0.2 cannot be represented precisely. Their sum becomes 0.30000000000000004 instead of 0.3, so the comparison returns false. Use techniques like `toFixed()` or `Math.round()` to handle precision safely.

blogs.title: why type of null is object? and even after that why null == null returns true?
blogs.shortDesc: `typeof null` returns 'object' due to a historical bug in the language specification. Despite this, null represents the intentional absence of an object, so comparing null to itself returns true because both are identical primitive values.

blogs.title: typeof NaN
blogs.shortDesc: NaN stands for Not-a-Number and represents an invalid numeric result. `typeof NaN` returns 'number' because NaN is still classified under the number type. Interestingly, NaN is not equal to itself (`NaN == NaN` is false), requiring `Number.isNaN()` for correct detection.

Comparing arrays using `==` returns false because arrays are reference-based objects. Even if they contain the same data, both arrays point to different memory locations, so they are not considered equal.

Like arrays, objects are compared by reference, not by structure. Two separate object literals never share the same reference, so equality comparison returns false. However, `{}` alone in the console can throw syntax confusion due to interpretation as a block rather than an object literal.

blogs.title: How do you handle large-scale application architecture in JavaScript?
blogs.shortDesc: Scaling applications requires modularization using patterns like MVC, MVVM, or micro-frontends; splitting code with ES Modules; dependency management; using services and DI; and optimizing performance with caching, lazy loading, and proper folder structure to ensure maintainability and growth.

blogs.title: What is the difference between Object.freeze() and Object.seal()?
blogs.shortDesc: `Object.freeze()` prevents any modification to an object—no new properties, no deletion, and no value changes. `Object.seal()` prevents adding or removing properties, but existing property values can still be edited. Freeze ensures complete immutability; seal allows controlled updates.

blogs.title: Explain the role of V8 engine in JavaScript execution.
blogs.shortDesc: V8 is Google’s high-performance JavaScript engine that compiles JavaScript directly into native machine code using JIT (Just-In-Time) compilation. It includes an optimizing compiler (TurboFan), garbage collector (Orinoco), and advanced memory management to maximize execution speed.

blogs.title: How to minimize reflows/repaints?
blogs.shortDesc: Reflows and repaints affect rendering performance. Minimize layout changes, batch DOM operations, use `requestAnimationFrame()`, apply `will-change` carefully, avoid layout thrashing, and use CSS transforms instead of changing layout properties like width or top.

blogs.title: What's the difference between Promise.all, Promise.race, Promise.allSettled, and Promise.any in terms of error handling?
blogs.shortDesc: `Promise.all()` fails if any promise rejects; `Promise.race()` resolves or rejects with the first settled result; `Promise.allSettled()` waits for all promises and gives results regardless of success or failure; `Promise.any()` resolves with the first fulfilled promise and only rejects if all fail.

blogs.title: How does prototype chain resolution work in JavaScript?
blogs.shortDesc: When accessing an object property, JavaScript looks first on the object itself, then on its prototype, and continues upward through the prototype chain until it finds the property or reaches `null`. This mechanism enables inheritance and shared behavior.

blogs.title: How does React handle event delegation internally?
blogs.shortDesc: React attaches a single event listener at the root using the synthetic event system. Events bubble up to the root listener, allowing React to manage them efficiently, normalize browser behavior, and improve performance rather than attaching listeners to every component.

blogs.title: What are proxies and how can they be used to intercept operations?
blogs.shortDesc: The Proxy API allows intercepting and controlling fundamental operations such as get, set, delete, and function calls. Proxies enable validations, logging, access control, reactivity systems (like Vue), and custom behavior wrapping around objects.

blogs.title: What's the use case for Reflect API?
blogs.shortDesc: Reflect provides a set of utility methods that simplify object manipulation and meta-programming. It works closely with Proxies and ensures predictable, standardized object operations like property access, assignment, and construction.

blogs.title: What's Generators and Iterators
blogs.shortDesc: Iterators define a controlled way of producing values sequentially. Generators simplify iterator creation using the `function*` syntax and `yield`, allowing pausing and resuming execution, making them useful for streaming, async flow control, and infinite sequences.

blogs.title: Explain how RxJS observables differ from Promises.
blogs.shortDesc: Promises handle a single final value and cannot be canceled, whereas Observables emit multiple values over time, support cancellation, and allow powerful operators for event streaming, real-time data handling, and complex async workflows.

blogs.title: How to add security in javascript
blogs.shortDesc: Securing JavaScript requires validating input, sanitizing user data, preventing XSS and CSRF attacks, using Content-Security-Policy, avoiding eval(), storing secrets on the server, hashing passwords, and following secure coding practices.

blogs.title: Create a tiny event bus system (pub/sub).
blogs.shortDesc: A pub/sub system lets components communicate indirectly via event publishing and subscribing. Publishers broadcast events, and subscribers listen to them without tight coupling. You can implement it using callbacks, maps of event types, and triggering listener functions.

blogs.title: How do you enforce type safety in vanilla JS?
blogs.shortDesc: You can achieve type safety using runtime validation, JSDoc annotations, strict mode, schema validators like Joi/Zod, TypeScript tooling, or defensive programming techniques to prevent invalid input and runtime errors.

blogs.title: What are the trade-offs of using TypeScript in large-scale projects?
blogs.shortDesc: TypeScript improves maintainability, tooling, and safety but adds compilation overhead, a learning curve, extra config, and sometimes slows prototyping. Large teams benefit the most due to reduced bug risk and better predictability.

blogs.title: How webpack / vite works
blogs.shortDesc: Webpack bundles all assets into files optimized for production with loaders and plugins. Vite uses ES modules in development and bundles only for production, providing faster startup through on-demand compilation and hot-module replacement.

blogs.title: CSS performance pipeline
blogs.shortDesc: Browsers parse CSS, compute styles, build render and layout trees, and then paint pixels on the screen. Efficient CSS structure improves rendering speed; heavy selectors, reflows, and repaints slow performance significantly.

blogs.title: How to minimize reflows and repaints in the browser?
blogs.shortDesc: Batch DOM reads and writes, avoid forcing synchronous layout, use transforms and opacity instead of layout changes, minimize complex CSS rules, group animations in layers, and use requestAnimationFrame for smoother rendering.

blogs.title: Why is will-change used, and what are the risks of overusing it?
blogs.shortDesc: `will-change` tells the browser that an element will likely be animated or changed soon, allowing it to optimize rendering layers. However, overuse wastes memory and can degrade performance instead of improving it.

blogs.title: What is the difference between hardware acceleration and normal rendering?
blogs.shortDesc: Normal rendering uses the CPU for layout and painting, while hardware acceleration uses the GPU for smoother processing of transforms, animations, and 3D effects. It enhances performance but must be used carefully to avoid memory pressure.

blogs.title: What is Node.js and how does it work?
blogs.shortDesc: Node.js is a JavaScript runtime built on Google's V8 engine that allows developers to run JavaScript outside the browser. It uses an event-driven, non-blocking I/O model which makes it efficient for building scalable network applications such as APIs, real-time systems, and microservices.

blogs.title: What is Event Driven Architecture in Node.js?
blogs.shortDesc: Node.js follows an event-driven architecture where operations emit events and listeners handle them asynchronously. The EventEmitter module allows components to communicate using events, enabling efficient handling of I/O-heavy applications without blocking the main thread.

blogs.title: What are Streams in Node.js?
blogs.shortDesc: Streams are objects that allow reading or writing data continuously instead of loading everything into memory at once. They are useful for handling large files, network communication, and real-time data processing. Examples include readable streams, writable streams, duplex streams, and transform streams.

blogs.title: What is Cluster module in Node.js?
blogs.shortDesc: The cluster module allows Node.js applications to create multiple worker processes that share the same server port. This helps utilize multiple CPU cores, improving performance and scalability for high-traffic applications.

blogs.title: process.nextTick() vs setImmediate()
blogs.shortDesc: Both functions schedule asynchronous execution but behave differently in the event loop. process.nextTick() runs immediately after the current operation before the event loop continues, while setImmediate() runs during the check phase after I/O callbacks.

blogs.title: What is Express.js?
blogs.shortDesc: Express.js is a minimal and flexible web framework built on top of Node.js. It simplifies server creation, routing, middleware management, and API development, making it one of the most widely used frameworks for building backend services and RESTful APIs.

blogs.title: What is Middleware in Express?
blogs.shortDesc: Middleware functions are functions that execute during the request-response cycle in Express. They can modify the request or response objects, execute code, end the request-response cycle, or pass control to the next middleware using the next() function.

blogs.title: How does Routing work in Express?
blogs.shortDesc: Routing in Express defines how an application responds to client requests at specific endpoints. Routes are defined using HTTP methods like GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE, allowing developers to organize API endpoints and handle different types of requests efficiently.

blogs.title: Error Handling in Express
blogs.shortDesc: Express handles errors using special middleware functions with four parameters: err, req, res, and next. These middleware functions capture errors from routes or other middleware and allow centralized error management for logging, formatting responses, and preventing crashes.

blogs.title: REST API Best Practices
blogs.shortDesc: Designing a good REST API involves using proper HTTP methods, meaningful endpoint naming, status codes, authentication mechanisms, pagination, versioning, and structured responses. Following REST principles ensures scalability and maintainability of backend services.

blogs.title: What is PostgreSQL?
blogs.shortDesc: PostgreSQL is a powerful open-source relational database known for reliability, advanced features, and SQL standards compliance. It supports complex queries, indexing, transactions, JSON data types, and extensions, making it suitable for large-scale and high-performance applications.

blogs.title: What are Indexes in PostgreSQL?
blogs.shortDesc: Indexes improve query performance by allowing the database to locate rows faster instead of scanning the entire table. PostgreSQL supports different types of indexes such as B-tree, Hash, GiST, and GIN, each optimized for specific query patterns.

blogs.title: What are Transactions in PostgreSQL?
blogs.shortDesc: Transactions ensure that a group of database operations execute reliably using the ACID properties: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability. They guarantee that either all operations succeed or none are applied, maintaining data integrity.

blogs.title: Types of Joins in PostgreSQL
blogs.shortDesc: Joins combine rows from multiple tables based on related columns. Common types include INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, and FULL JOIN, each used to retrieve relational data in different ways depending on the required result set.

blogs.title: What is Database Normalization?
blogs.shortDesc: Normalization is the process of structuring a database to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity. It involves dividing tables into smaller related tables and defining relationships using primary and foreign keys.

blogs.title: Node.js Event Loop Deep Dive
blogs.shortDesc: The Node.js event loop is responsible for handling asynchronous operations in a single-threaded environment. It works through different phases such as timers, pending callbacks, idle/prepare, poll, check, and close callbacks. Understanding these phases helps developers write more efficient and non-blocking applications.

blogs.title: Worker Threads vs Cluster in Node.js
blogs.shortDesc: Node.js provides worker threads and cluster modules to utilize multiple CPU cores. Worker threads run JavaScript in parallel threads sharing memory, while the cluster module creates multiple processes that share the same server port, making them suitable for different performance optimization scenarios.

blogs.title: Memory Management in Node.js
blogs.shortDesc: Node.js manages memory through the V8 engine using a garbage collection system. It allocates memory for objects in the heap and automatically frees unused memory. Understanding memory leaks, heap size, and garbage collection behavior is crucial for building high-performance applications.

blogs.title: Rate Limiting in Node.js APIs
blogs.shortDesc: Rate limiting restricts how many requests a client can make within a specified time frame. It helps prevent abuse, DDoS attacks, and server overload. Libraries like express-rate-limit allow developers to implement rate limiting easily in Node.js APIs.

blogs.title: Caching Strategies in Node.js
blogs.shortDesc: Caching improves performance by storing frequently accessed data temporarily. Node.js applications can use in-memory caching, Redis caching, or HTTP caching headers to reduce database queries and speed up API responses.

blogs.title: Authentication using JWT in Express
blogs.shortDesc: JWT authentication allows secure user authentication without maintaining server-side sessions. After login, the server generates a signed token that the client includes in future requests. Middleware verifies the token to authorize access to protected routes.

blogs.title: Express Security Best Practices
blogs.shortDesc: Securing Express applications involves using Helmet for HTTP security headers, input validation, rate limiting, HTTPS enforcement, CORS configuration, and protecting against common attacks like XSS, CSRF, and SQL injection.

blogs.title: MVC Architecture in Express Applications
blogs.shortDesc: MVC stands for Model-View-Controller and is used to structure backend applications. Models manage data, controllers handle business logic, and routes connect requests to controllers. This separation improves maintainability and scalability.

blogs.title: Express Request Lifecycle
blogs.shortDesc: When a request reaches an Express server, it passes through middleware, route handlers, and response processing stages. Understanding this lifecycle helps developers manage authentication, validation, logging, and error handling effectively.

blogs.title: Handling File Uploads in Express
blogs.shortDesc: File uploads in Express are typically handled using middleware like Multer. It parses multipart/form-data requests and allows storing uploaded files locally or in cloud storage services like AWS S3.

blogs.title: PostgreSQL Query Optimization Techniques
blogs.shortDesc: Optimizing PostgreSQL queries involves proper indexing, avoiding unnecessary joins, analyzing execution plans with EXPLAIN, and reducing full-table scans. These techniques significantly improve database performance for large datasets.

blogs.title: Using JSON and JSONB in PostgreSQL
blogs.shortDesc: PostgreSQL supports JSON and JSONB data types that allow storing semi-structured data inside relational tables. JSONB provides better indexing and faster querying, making it useful for flexible schemas and modern applications.

blogs.title: Different Types of Indexes in PostgreSQL
blogs.shortDesc: PostgreSQL supports multiple index types including B-tree, Hash, GIN, GiST, and BRIN. Each index type is optimized for specific queries such as text search, range queries, or large datasets.

blogs.title: Connection Pooling in PostgreSQL
blogs.shortDesc: Connection pooling improves database performance by reusing existing connections instead of creating new ones for every request. Tools like pg-pool or PgBouncer manage connection pools efficiently in production environments.

blogs.title: Locking Mechanisms in PostgreSQL
blogs.shortDesc: PostgreSQL uses row-level and table-level locks to maintain data consistency during concurrent transactions. Understanding locking behavior helps avoid deadlocks and improves database reliability.

blogs.title: Database Migrations Explained
blogs.shortDesc: Database migrations allow developers to version-control database schema changes over time. Tools like Sequelize, Knex, or Prisma migrations ensure that schema updates are consistent across development, staging, and production environments.

blogs.title: API Versioning Strategies
blogs.shortDesc: API versioning ensures backward compatibility when updating APIs. Common strategies include URL versioning, header versioning, and query parameter versioning, allowing developers to evolve APIs without breaking existing clients.

blogs.title: Microservices vs Monolithic Architecture
blogs.shortDesc: Monolithic architecture builds the application as a single codebase, while microservices break functionality into independent services. Each approach has advantages depending on scalability, team size, and system complexity.

blogs.title: API Pagination Techniques
blogs.shortDesc: Pagination improves API performance by limiting how many records are returned per request. Common approaches include offset pagination, cursor-based pagination, and keyset pagination, each suited for different types of data retrieval.

blogs.title: Logging and Monitoring Backend APIs
blogs.shortDesc: Monitoring APIs involves logging requests, tracking errors, and measuring performance metrics. Tools like Winston, Morgan, Prometheus, and Grafana help maintain reliability and quickly detect issues in production systems.

blogs.title: What are Closures in JavaScript?
blogs.shortDesc: A closure is created when a function remembers variables from its outer scope even after the outer function has finished executing. Closures allow functions to maintain private state and are commonly used for data encapsulation, callbacks, and functional programming patterns.

blogs.title: What is Hoisting in JavaScript?
blogs.shortDesc: Hoisting is JavaScript's default behavior of moving declarations to the top of the current scope before execution. Variables declared with var are hoisted with undefined values, while let and const are hoisted but remain in the temporal dead zone until initialization.

blogs.title: call vs apply vs bind in JavaScript
blogs.shortDesc: call, apply, and bind are methods used to control the value of 'this' when invoking functions. call invokes a function immediately with arguments passed individually, apply accepts arguments as an array, and bind returns a new function with 'this' permanently bound.

blogs.title: Debounce vs Throttle in JavaScript
blogs.shortDesc: Debouncing delays function execution until a certain time has passed since the last event, while throttling limits the function execution to once within a specified interval. These techniques improve performance when handling frequent events like scrolling or typing.

blogs.title: Deep Copy vs Shallow Copy in JavaScript
blogs.shortDesc: A shallow copy duplicates only the first level of an object while nested objects remain referenced. A deep copy duplicates all nested objects as well, ensuring complete independence between the copied and original objects.

blogs.title: What is Virtual DOM in React?
blogs.shortDesc: The Virtual DOM is a lightweight in-memory representation of the real DOM. React updates the virtual DOM first, compares it with the previous version using a diffing algorithm, and then updates only the changed parts of the actual DOM, improving performance.

blogs.title: What are React Hooks?
blogs.shortDesc: Hooks are functions introduced in React that allow functional components to use state and lifecycle features without writing class components. Examples include useState, useEffect, useContext, and useReducer which simplify component logic and improve code reusability.

blogs.title: How does useEffect work in React?
blogs.shortDesc: useEffect is a React hook used to perform side effects in functional components such as API calls, subscriptions, or DOM updates. It runs after the component renders and can optionally depend on specific variables using the dependency array.

blogs.title: Controlled vs Uncontrolled Components in React
blogs.shortDesc: Controlled components rely on React state to manage form input values, while uncontrolled components store form data directly in the DOM using refs. Controlled components provide better control and validation, making them preferred in most React applications.

blogs.title: React Memoization (React.memo, useMemo, useCallback)
blogs.shortDesc: Memoization in React helps avoid unnecessary re-renders by caching computed values or components. React.memo prevents component re-renders when props haven't changed, while useMemo and useCallback cache values and functions respectively.

blogs.title: What are Semantic HTML Elements?
blogs.shortDesc: Semantic HTML elements clearly describe their meaning to both developers and browsers. Tags like header, footer, article, section, and nav improve accessibility, SEO, and maintainability by giving structure and meaning to the content.

blogs.title: LocalStorage vs SessionStorage
blogs.shortDesc: LocalStorage and SessionStorage are Web Storage APIs used to store key-value data in the browser. LocalStorage persists data even after the browser is closed, while SessionStorage stores data only for the duration of the current browser session.

blogs.title: async vs defer in script tags
blogs.shortDesc: The async and defer attributes control how JavaScript files load in HTML. async loads scripts asynchronously and executes immediately when ready, while defer loads scripts in the background but executes them only after the HTML document is fully parsed.

blogs.title: What is Flexbox in CSS?
blogs.shortDesc: Flexbox is a layout module that provides an efficient way to align and distribute space among items in a container. It allows flexible positioning, alignment, and ordering of elements using properties like justify-content, align-items, and flex-direction.

blogs.title: What is CSS Grid?
blogs.shortDesc: CSS Grid is a two-dimensional layout system that allows developers to create complex layouts using rows and columns. It provides precise control over placement of elements using grid-template-rows, grid-template-columns, and grid-area properties.

blogs.title: What is CSS Specificity?
blogs.shortDesc: CSS specificity determines which CSS rule is applied when multiple rules target the same element. It is calculated based on selector types such as inline styles, IDs, classes, attributes, and element selectors.

blogs.title: What is Utility-First CSS in Tailwind?
blogs.shortDesc: Tailwind CSS follows a utility-first approach where small, single-purpose classes are used directly in HTML to style elements. This eliminates the need for writing custom CSS and enables faster UI development with consistent design patterns.

blogs.title: Responsive Design in Tailwind CSS
blogs.shortDesc: Tailwind CSS provides built-in responsive design utilities using breakpoint prefixes such as sm, md, lg, and xl. These utilities allow developers to apply different styles at different screen sizes without writing custom media queries.

blogs.title: How to Customize Tailwind CSS?
blogs.shortDesc: Tailwind can be customized using the tailwind.config.js file where developers can extend colors, spacing, typography, breakpoints, and plugins. This allows teams to maintain a consistent design system across large applications.
